title: Adhesive on Polyester
stainChemistry: Adhesives on polyester split into four classes: (1) rubber / contact — petroleum solvent (POG); (2) acrylic / white / school — IPA or warm NSD; (3) cyanoacrylate / super glue — acetone, but acetone destroys acetate blends so verify fiber; (4) hot-melt — freeze, pick, treat residue. Polyester is solvent-tolerant at room temperature and heat-sensitive above 170°F.
whyThisWorks: Glue bonds to polyester through Van der Waals and mechanical adhesion to fiber texture — dissolve the polymer and the bond goes with it. IPA lifts most acrylic/white-glue residues. NSD + gentle tamping lifts water-soluble residue. Acetone reserved for confirmed cyanoacrylate on confirmed polyester (not acetate). Reading the adhesive class first decides the path.
defaultAssumption: Assume water-soluble or acrylic adhesive unless the user says otherwise. Cyanoacrylate gets called out explicitly if they mention super glue.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Ask: what kind? Super glue → acetone, confirm polyester (not acetate). Rubber cement → POG. White / school / acrylic → IPA. Hot-melt → chill, pick, treat residue. Remove pickable bulk with a dull blade before wetting anything.
Cold water flush from the back of the fabric. Loosens water-soluble glue and pushes mobilized residue back out the way it came in.
NSD directly on the spot. Tamp with a bristle brush. Water-soluble adhesives lift here.
If residue persists, switch to isopropyl alcohol on a white cloth. Tamp — do not pour. Flip cloth frequently. IPA handles most acrylic and white-glue residues polyester tolerates.
Flush the spot clean. Blot. Inspect. No heat until residue is gone — heat sets uncured adhesive into the fiber.
Repeat steps 3–5 once if residue remains. Confirmed super glue on confirmed polyester: acetone on a cotton swab, tamp from back, fast flush. Never acetone on acetate.

steps: 1. ID adhesive class. Scrape pickable bulk.
4. If residue remains: IPA on white cloth, tamp (not pour). Flip cloth.
5. Rinse cool. Blot. No heat until clean.
6. Repeat NSD+IPA once. For confirmed super glue on confirmed polyester: acetone on swab, tamp from back, fast flush.
professionalProtocol.temperature: room temperature — no heat until residue is fully gone
warnings: Never use acetone on acetate — dissolves the fiber. Verify polyester first.
Never heat an uncured or residual adhesive — cures it into the fiber permanently.
Test every solvent on an inside seam on dyed or printed polyester.

homeSolutions.instruction: Different glues need different removers — skip this step and you'll guess wrong. Super glue? Acetone-based nail polish remover (only on real polyester, not acetate blends — acetone dissolves acetate). School / white glue? Warm water + dish soap. Rubber cement? Goo Gone or orange oil. Hot glue? Freeze the fabric and pick it off once brittle.

Before wetting anything, scrape off solid or rubbery bulk with the edge of a credit card or a dull butter knife. Don't pull hard on the fabric — if it doesn't come free easily, leave it for the solvent step.

homeSolutions.agent: White / school glue → warm water + Dawn
homeSolutions.instruction: Water-soluble glues give up fast. Run warm water through the back of the stain for 60 seconds, then work a drop of Dawn into the spot with a fingertip. Let it sit 5 minutes, rinse. Two rounds clears most of it.

homeSolutions.agent: Super glue → acetone on a cotton swab
homeSolutions.instruction: Confirm the garment is actually polyester and not an acetate blend — acetone dissolves acetate. Check the label. Dab acetone-based nail polish remover on a cotton swab from the BACK of the fabric, dab-and-blot, don't pour. Super glue lifts in seconds. Flush cold water immediately after.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubber cement / contact glue → citrus or oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply Goo Gone, orange oil, or cooking oil to the residue. Let it sit 10 minutes — the adhesive gets gummy. Work gently with a soft toothbrush, lift dissolved residue with a paper towel. Wash the oil out with Dawn + warm water.
Put the fabric in the freezer 20 minutes. Hot glue turns brittle when frozen and usually pops off with light pressure from a dull blade. Any film left behind goes through the Dawn + warm water pass.

Let the fabric air-dry fully before throwing it in the dryer. Any adhesive residue will cure permanently under dryer heat. If you still see or feel a tacky spot, repeat the solvent step that matched your glue before drying.

steps: 1. Identify the glue: super glue, school glue, rubber cement, or hot glue — different removers for each.
2. Scrape off any solid bulk with the edge of a credit card.
3. Super glue on polyester (not acetate): dab acetone-based nail polish remover on a cotton swab from the back of the fabric.
4. White / school / water-soluble glue: warm water + a drop of Dawn, work in, rinse.
5. Rubber / contact cement: Goo Gone or orange oil, 10-minute dwell, soft-brush lift, wash out with Dawn.
6. Hot glue: freeze the fabric, pick the brittle glue off, clean residue with warm water + Dawn.
7. Air-dry fully before the dryer — heat cures residual adhesive permanently.
products: Acetone-based nail polish remover (super glue only)
Goo Gone or orange oil (rubber / contact adhesive)
A dull blade or credit card edge
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the glue has been through a hot dryer or iron, if the fabric label says acetate and the glue is super glue, or if two home rounds haven't helped. Take it to a dry cleaner with the adhesive type identified.
warnings: Never use acetone on acetate — dissolves the fiber
Never apply heat until the stain is fully gone — heat cures residual adhesive permanently
Test solvents on an inside seam first on colored or printed polyester

neverDo: Never use acetone on acetate fibers — dissolves them
Never apply heat, iron, or dryer until residue is fully gone — cures adhesive permanently
Never pour solvent onto fabric — tamp with a cloth
Never mix acetone with other solvents
fiberSensitivities: Polyester tolerates most common adhesive solvents at room temperature
Acetone safe on pure polyester, destroys acetate — verify blend before use
Heat-sensitive above 170°F — avoid iron, hot dryer, hair-dryer drying

materialWarnings: Verify polyester vs acetate on the care label before acetone
No heat until residue is fully gone
Test all solvents on an inside seam first

escalation.when: If the adhesive has been heat-set, the label is acetate and the glue is cyanoacrylate, or two home rounds haven't cleared it — take it to a dry cleaner.
escalation.whatToTell: Name the adhesive type if known: "super glue / rubber cement / school glue / hot glue on polyester, treated with [solvent] — no heat applied." Gives the pro the exact solvent class to reach for.

contextQuestions: What kind of glue is it — super glue, school glue, rubber cement, hot glue, something else?
Is the fabric 100% polyester or a blend (especially with acetate)?
Has the garment been through a dryer or iron since the glue hit it?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Adhesive class decides the solvent path — super glue = acetone (polyester only), white glue = water/Dawn, rubber cement = citrus/oil, hot glue = freeze. Polyester vs acetate blend is the critical label check before acetone.
